structural_claim: Environmental boundary conditions fundamentally shape quantum processes and spontaneous emission rates through vacuum fluctuation modification

supporting_results.structural_conclusion: Environmental entanglement dynamically selects preferred pointer states, explaining the emergence of classical behavior through information dispersal
The electromagnetic environment modifies spontaneous emission rates, revealing that vacuum fluctuations and thus fundamental quantum optical processes depend on boundary conditions
Single atoms and photons can coherently exchange energy faster than dissipation, establishing a regime where light-matter interaction becomes a reversible quantum process
